aliases: Vietnamese rice noodle soup with beef

english: Vietnamese rice noodle soup with beef

food_subgroup: Clear beef bone broth soup with flat rice noodles (banh pho), sliced beef brisket, tendon, bean sprouts, and fresh herbs

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Pho Bo. Closest parent is a Southeast Asian rice noodle soup dish. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vietnamese beef bone broth soup with flat rice noodles (banh pho), sliced beef brisket, tendon, bean sprouts, and fresh herbs
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0BQB is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a Southeast Asian noodle soup with meat. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Pho Bo specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Pho Bo as a specific food item.

preparation_method: assembled bowl with raw-finish sliced beef

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard full bowl of Pho Bo weighs approximately 600g, comprising roughly 300g beef bone broth, 150g flat rice noodles, 100g sliced beef brisket and tendon, and 50g garnishes including bean sprouts, Thai basil, spring onion, and coriander.

nutrient_source.title: Pho Bo (Vietnamese Beef Noodle Soup) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table for Vietnamese beef noodle soup (Pho Bo). Values represent the full assembled bowl per 100g edible portion including broth, noodles, beef, and garnishes. Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central component data for beef brisket (FDC ID 174032), cooked rice noodles (FDC ID 168917), and beef bone broth to validate macro plausibility. The composite per-100g values are consistent with a 600g bowl containing approximately 300g broth, 150g noodles, 100g beef, and 50g garnishes.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Rice noodles (flat, banh pho type)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Flat rice noodles (banh pho) have a published GI of approximately 60–68 in the University of Sydney GI Database and associated peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). A midpoint GI of 62 is applied to the assembled Pho Bo bowl. The clear broth contributes negligible carbohydrate; the noodle fraction dominates the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 600g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (62 × 58.8) / 100 = 36.5. Carbohydrate per serving = 9.8g/100g × 600g = 58.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 382mg per 100g; a full 600g bowl delivers approximately 2,292mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce and beef bone broth seasoning, exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit of 2,000mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should leave 30–50% of the Pho broth uneaten, as sodium is concentrated in the broth rather than the noodles or beef; requesting reduced fish sauce when ordering and consuming the noodles and beef while discarding most of the broth can reduce sodium intake to approximately 1,400–1,600mg per bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load of 36.5 per full 600g serving from flat rice noodles (GI 62); however, the substantial lean beef protein content (5.5g/100g) and collagen-rich bone broth gelatin meaningfully attenuate post-prandial glucose response compared to most noodle-based dishes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Pho Bo in moderation by requesting a smaller noodle portion (reducing GL to approximately 20–25), ensuring adequate protein from the beef brisket and tendon, and avoiding added hoisin sauce which contributes additional sugar; monitoring post-prandial glucose is recommended for those on insulin or sulfonylureas.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content (0.5g/100g) and lean beef protein profile are cardiovascular-favorable; however, the high sodium load (382mg/100g) poses a meaningful risk for blood pressure elevation in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Pho Bo can be a relatively heart-favorable noodle dish due to its low saturated fat and lean protein, but sodium management is critical — consuming only 30–50% of the broth and requesting reduced fish sauce are the most effective strategies to limit cardiovascular sodium risk while retaining the nutritional benefits of the beef and noodles.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Long-simmered beef bone broth is rich in collagen-derived gelatin, hydroxyproline, and proline — precursors for cartilage synthesis; aromatic spices including cinnamon and star anise (anethole) have documented anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pho Bo is a clinically favorable choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its collagen-rich bone broth, anti-inflammatory spice profile (star anise, cinnamon), lean beef providing zinc (1.5mg/100g) and vitamin B12 for immune function, and bean sprouts and Thai basil contributing vitamin C and anti-inflammatory flavonoids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pho Bo (Phở Bò) is Vietnam's most celebrated national dish — a crystal-clear, richly fragrant beef bone broth soup that requires 8–12 hours of careful preparation and represents one of the most complex and revered soups in the Asian culinary tradition. The broth is built by first charring beef bones (knuckle, marrow, and oxtail) and aromatics (ginger and onion) to develop deep caramelized flavor, then simmering at a gentle rolling boil for many hours with whole spices including star anise, cinnamon sticks, cloves, black cardamom pods, and rock sugar. The result is a broth that is simultaneously clear, deeply beefy, subtly sweet, and warmly spiced. The assembled bowl is served over flat rice noodles (bánh phở) with thinly sliced beef brisket, tender tendon pieces, spring onion, and fresh coriander, accompanied by a garnish plate of bean sprouts, Thai basil, lime wedges, hoisin sauce, and Sriracha. The ritual of customizing the bowl — tearing in herbs, squeezing lime, adding condiments — is central to the pho dining experience. Nutritionally, Pho Bo is one of the more balanced restaurant noodle soups: lean beef provides 5.5g protein per 100g, saturated fat is low at 0.5g/100g, and the bone broth is rich in collagen precursors (hydroxyproline, proline, glycine) with potential benefits for joint health. The primary nutritional concern is sodium at 382mg/100g (approximately 2,292mg per full 600g bowl), derived from fish sauce and seasoned broth. The glycemic index is moderate at 62, driven by the flat rice noodles, with a glycemic load of 36.5 per serving. In Singapore, Pho Bo is widely available at Vietnamese restaurants across the city and is a popular lunch and dinner option. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified fish sauce and beef.
meal_context: popular lunch and dinner Vietnamese option

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table, an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes, with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central component data for beef brisket and cooked rice noodles. GI value derived from University of Sydney GI Database for flat rice noodles (banh pho type), an approved GI-specific source. All values are internally consistent and plausible for the described composite dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the assembled Pho Bo composite dish (broth + noodles + beef + garnishes) is not available in the FAO/INFOODS SEA table or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the assembled composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish; lean beef and rice noodle composition does not contribute meaningful trans fat. The glycemic load of 36.5 per 600g serving is calculated as (62 × 58.8) / 100, where 58.8g carbohydrate = 9.8g/100g × 600g serving. Sodium is concentrated in the broth fraction; practical sodium reduction is achievable by leaving 30–50% of broth uneaten. The FoodEx2 code A0BQB is used as the closest defensible parent category for a Southeast Asian noodle soup with meat; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Pho Bo specifically. FoodOn URI is null as no specific FoodOn concept exists for Pho Bo — closest parent is a Southeast Asian rice noodle soup. The input record listed ethnic_tags as 'western' which appears to be a data entry error; this has been corrected to 'Vietnamese' in the output. Two duplicate default portions were present in the input; consolidated to a single canonical 600g bowl serving.
